item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like it overall, but one part clanged for me: the list of materials 015's pipes are made from and the following contrasting list of "traditional" materials. Steel is actually a pretty common material for pipes (you'll see it used for natural gas, for example, often called "black iron pipe"). I feel like that should be taken off the list of 015 pipe materials. Maybe replace with "an unidentified metal alloy" and/or "a ceramic of unknown composition"? (Since obviously anyone who tries to go in and scrape off a sample for analysis, or even bring in equipment to determine composition of the pipe in place, will wish they hadn't.) These material categories could also plausibly hold liquid iron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of The Backrooms, which feel oddly nostalgic to me. Definitely deserving of an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
015, insert screensaver joke here!
It’s been mentioned before, but there are a lot of innocuous piles of stuff that try to eat and grow. Sure, it makes sense, but it’s the same sense that lead to the evolution of a bobcat. Sure, it’s cool and all, but I’ve already seen panthers, and those worked basically the same.
What pushed this one up, for me, was realising that it’s goal isn’t to grow, it’s to connect, be part of a pre-established system, even though it’s unsuitable for it. I can sympathise with that.
On it’s own, it’s alright, but the tales are kinda necessary for full enjoyment. Still, I’m going to upvote, but It sets a standard that other eat’n’grows have to be judged by.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how it feels about people trying to polish it, or sweep up around it.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
GeoLikesLogic, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

If you would like a post disregarded, just edit it to note that.

item.title: Re: the absolute worst thing you could ever do
item.content:encoded: okay i've just learnt that you can't delete your own posts so that sucks

item.title: the absolute worst thing you could ever do
item.content:encoded: put 184 in this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is a nice idea, I think the tale is what makes it though. It may not be the best written of tales dialogue-wise I've seen, but it conjures up really good imagery of the weird crap inside SCP-15 and why you really really shouldn't piss it off! I think this one has stood the test of time pretty well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this one is honestly one of my favorite SCPs ever! its so ominous and weirdly I feel sympathy for it?? all its trying to do is defend itself (I guess). If I close my eyes I can hear the ambience with the bangs and gurgles and steam hisses and screams. just very cool!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… it's a maze of pipes that doesn't condone fighting, tool, or changes made to it?
That sounds pretty awesome, actually. A little deadly to some people, but still awesome.
Heck, I wouldn't mind going through there, with the right supplement.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

With how brief this article, adding a crosslink that is not in-line, most likely will come across as a little contrived, so in-line is the way to go. Turns out Gears wrote a pretty neat tale called Plumbing about what happens to a few SCP Agents that accidentally damage a bit of 015's interior, which would make for a great crosslink within the description warning against damaging 015's pipes. As such, I am proposing the following edit:

Changing the following:

SCP-015 reacts to tools and aggression. Any personnel acting violently, carrying tools, or attempting to damage or repair SCP-015 in any way, will trigger a reaction. Any pipes near the subject will burst, spraying on the subject for several seconds before the flow suddenly stops. Pipes have been reported containing oil, mercury, rats, a species of insect not yet identified, ground glass, sea water, entrails, and molten iron. Pipes will continue to burst around subject until death or retreat.

To this:

SCP-015 reacts to tools and aggression. Any personnel acting violently, carrying tools, or attempting to damage or repair SCP-015 in any way, will trigger a reaction. Any pipes near the subject will burst, spraying on the subject for several seconds before the flow suddenly stops. Pipes have been reported containing oil, mercury, rats, a species of insect not yet identified, ground glass, sea water, entrails, and molten iron. Pipes will continue to burst around subject until death or retreat.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my blood's just run cold because i accidentally switched it back to the original page. but it's ok i fixed it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice to know I hit what I was aiming for ^_^
I had Gear's sequel-tale Plumbing running through my head while I was putting it together- that image of it as a quasi-organic, maybe even predatory, thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The image may feel less scary to me but it also feels more menacing, which I think helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This bugs me enough to necropost- 190 km of pipe is entirely reasonable. A typical mid-sized warehouse is about 10000 m2 (100 m x 100 m). That means it only takes ten pipes running the entire length to make a kilometer of pipe. 10*190=1900 end-to-end pipelengths.

If anything, 190 km of pipe indicates they've pruned it back to a fairly small warehouse, because even assuming a huge 20 cm diameter pipe as the average, that's only about 6000 m3 of volume, not counting any empty space between pipes. That volume could easily fit inside a 20 m x 20 m x 20 m cube.

Moral: If you're going to nitpick stuff like this, make sure you know what you're talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Late reply, but there's actually a pretty good explanation for the pipe materials if you think about it: it doesn't create its pipes from nothing. It requires raw materials, and it isn't picky about how it defines that phrase. Waste not, want not.

That also explains where all the stuff it's pumping goes. It's like a plant in all senses of the word. It's probably got roots running down to the mantle to bring up magma for internal kilns, crucibles, and boilers; extruders for making new pipes, chemical vats, breeding chambers for its bugs… you'd be amazed what you can do with few to no moving parts, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. You may want to PM Vincent_Redgrave as he's the one that pulled the image for CC violation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is perfect, IMO!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I made a thing if we're still wanting a replacement page image for this one:
My 'happy place' looks a little like this, incidentally.

Nothing too flashy here, just a photobash with a lot of paintover. It's CC compliant though- all the component clips came from my own stock.

For those interested, what I didn't paint started out as a tractor engine, an industrial generator, some rusty pipes, an airplane fuel truck, a couple different buildings, and a workover rig.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the 3d pipes screensaver (my favorite when I used Windows, before I got my Mac).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I hadn't seen that the picture was gone… hmm.
I'll see if I can't photobash something together with a similar vibe. I've got a lot of personal stock for industrial-type stuff, and this is a personal favorite among the old-school scips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't see it. There are mutliple issues that I have with article. For one, even if the final paragraph adds alittle creep factor to it, it honestly isn't very interesting. Why does this thing do what it does? Is it considered sentient (I think it would be)? Why is it made out of all those wierd materials instead of copper or iron? Most of the things that add the 'creep factor' to this article feel really gimicky. I don't really see a point in it. Even if the author wanted to keep the article 'light weight' and not bloated with information I feel like the article needs alittle bit more of an explaination. At the end of the day I end up asking myself why I should care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you should check the post edits for the real "first image", and I've seen it before as a screen-saver. No idea why'd anyone would use that. To me, the current "first" image actually looks plausible to serve a 015 picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second image looks like a diorama, with an obviously false background (or has the background been edited to look flat?). Which would kill it for me, unless it was cropped.

I am not sure why you think the first image looks like a screensaver. It is a photograph of some industrial process area.

None of these images seem especially spooky to me though; they look like where I work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really spooky mass of pipes. You can see the image in this YouTube video.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What was the image?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tacking more detail onto an SCP report is one of the easiest ways to bloat it. Really, the biggest issue with this one is the lack of a good image. The prior image (sadly, not CC-compliant) really made this piece for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really like this SCP, but I also really wish that more would be added onto it, like a few experimentation logs, addendum's, and an expanded containment procedure/description

item.title: Rating
item.content:encoded:
There are 2 problems with this article

1) The description says that it will start spraying the subject for a few seconds before stopping, but then states that it will not stop until the subject retreats

2) It says that it will spray anyone carrying "Tools", which I feel is a bit too ambiguous, what does the SCP consider to be "Tools"?

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely a no on the first image; it looks like one of those old self-drawing/generating screensavers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Staff don't allow uncollapsed images in the forums. The grayscale one seems promising if the licensing really does allow it, so you should totally contact the image team about it.

E: However, this site is CC 3.0, and I don't know the difference between that and 2.0 but you should bring it up with staff/whoever you talk to for licensing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Found two images that might serve 015 well. Checking with image staff about CC 3.0 v/s 2.0.

+ Show Images +
- Hide Images -

Listed as available under Creative Commons 2.0 Attributionl 2.0 Generic
Listed as available under Creative Commons 2.0 Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 Generic
(Probably Grayscale this one)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really needs a CC-compliant image. The old one made this thing really horrifying, and I think in this case, it'd really help accentuate the horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Back when I first read this one, before the image snafu, I would've upvoted it. Sadly, without an image, this one is kinda meh.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God; I read one of the previous comments about a human being connected to this, and now I can't stop thinking about a WH40K Dark Mechanicus-style withered husk somewhere at the center, using the pipe system to power itself somehow. The only thing stopping me from upvoting this is the "pipes have been reported as being made of bone, wood, steel, pressed ash, human flesh, glass, and granite" section. I know I defended the statement that the pipes often carry iron above, but having the pipes be constructed from bone, pressed ash, human flesh and granite feels completely unnecessary to me; it's creepy enough without the added squick and silliness, in my opinion. The stuff it carries is fun, though—where is it all being pumped, and why?

And, yeah, I'm going to agree with requests for a (separate) Exploration Log, if just to, y'know, follow the fluids.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The human flesh probably doesn't. I don't think the article needs to mention that specifically, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Naah, when I read it a second time, I began changing my vote to -1.
SCP-015 is completely filling a warehouse somewhere but SCP-015 contains, at current estimate, over 190 kilometers (120 miles) of pipes.
Lets say that a huge warehouse is approximately 80m (l) x 80m (w). The ware house is 0.0004 times smaller than SCP-015
Now imagine that this warehouse is being filled by a pipesystem with a length over 190 kilometers, just for one ███████ warehouse somewhere around the world.

Also it's made of bone, wood, steel, pressed ash, human flesh, glass, and granite.
Okay if SCP-015 reacts to tools and aggression, any pipes near the subject will burst, spraying on the subject for several seconds and if pipes have been reported containing oil, mercury, rats, a species of insect not yet identified, ground glass, sea water, entrails, and molten iron.
How the hell can human flesh hold molten iron???

This article is in my personal opinion not good.

item.title: Re: PLOT HOLE AHOY
item.content:encoded: Late comment, but this would make for some excellent tale fodder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh wow I remember this from one of the first times I stumbled upon SCPs, it seemed really cool back in the day but it has not stood the test of time well… Now it feels sort of unfullfilling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. +1.

item.title: Re: PLOT HOLE AHOY
item.content:encoded: It was cut down when the expansive nature of 015 had been isolated, but not its reaction to tools and alterations. It was not what you'd call easy, involving a lot of trial and error, remote actions, and quiet a few injurys and deaths. The Foundation doesn't always know how to deal with things right away…get it contained first, worry about safety after. In this case, stopping the expansion/infestation took priority over the odd frequency of burst pipes.

item.title: Re: PLOT HOLE AHOY
item.content:encoded: My guess? Explosives or armored vehicles of some sort. Respectively, stuff that renders short-range defenses irrelevant, and stuff that can take the stuff that the pipe nightmare throws at it. Perhaps "cut" isn't the right word.

item.title: PLOT HOLE AHOY
item.content:encoded:
Reread this for the first time in a while today.

No hand or power tools are allowed anywhere inside SCP-015. No repairs or maintenance are to be made anywhere on SCP-015.

SCP-015 reacts to tools and aggression. Any personnel acting violently, carrying tools, or attempting to damage or repair SCP-015 in any way, will trigger a reaction. Any pipes near the subject will burst, spraying on the subject for several seconds before the flow suddenly stops. Pipes have been reported containing oil, mercury, rats, a species of insect not yet identified, ground glass, sea water, entrails, and molten iron. Pipes will continue to burst around subject until death or retreat.

Man, this sure seems like an SCP that reacts very badly to people or instruments designed to cause change to the shape or function of its internal makeup. I guess there's no way for anybody to go in there and make a dramatic alteration to its size or interconnected natur —

SCP-015 was cut back to its current structure after attaching to 11 other structures in the area.

…how was it cut back without tools?

item.title: game
item.content:encoded: There is a beta fan-made game based of this scp, look it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The image in this article (original source here) has been temporarily removed for non-compliance with our licensing guidelines. Attempts to contact the original creator for usage permission or find a replacement are currently underway.

Please contact Accelerando or another member of the Image Team if you have any questions about this decision.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see this be a heritage article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Currently, 11 personnel have been killed, and 20 more are still missing. Reports have been made of banging and screaming coming from within SCP-015.

The best WTF in foundation history.

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Was going through the 100s block when I saw this creepy little gem. It's pretty good. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now +200.
Short, sweet, creepy as hell. 'Nuff said

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pipes have been reported containing oil, mercury, rats, a species of insect not yet identified, ground glass, sea water, entrails, and molten iron.

The first thing that comes to mind is that "sea water" is like that "one clown" in the old body count prank.

But then I realize the sea water is probably a bitch when it reacts with the wounds caused by everything else. The only way it could be worse is if this thing had lemon juice inside the pipes sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Much like other SCPs of the kind(bizarre growing/live/non-euclidian structures that react to their surroundings), this would really welcome an exploration log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice, but it would be a lot better with an exploration log attached to it.

No vote from me.

item.title: That's not so bad though
item.content:encoded:
The universe of the SCP Foundation is a world with so many strange/unpredictable forms of life.

If there are multiple planets and dimensions(not to mention human's long unwritten past) the whole concept of "eats things and grows" would just be another path the aliens', or even earthlings', evolution took.

Just like seeing a bunch of species of shark or big cats make you think: "Ugh! how bout some something else for a change!", just think of it as another phyla in the animal kingdom or something.

item.title: whoops
item.content:encoded:
Sorry about that. I kind of blundered my way into disaster over this and didn't realize it until getting messaged earlier this evening. Explained here - http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-225702/scp-426#post-1522360

If anybody's interested, here is the supplementary image I created for the page, with the caption "Frame #1171-12J of nightvision footage from Class-D personnel headcam, recovered following May 2006 deep-exploration. Area depicted was previously unseen.".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Paradigm, edits beyond basic formatting/typos need to be cleared with the original author (or Staff, assuming the original author is no longer active on the wiki).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reverted. Don't add images to SCPs that aren't yours without permission. Also, this was doing just fine without a second image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a second image. Hope people like it. Somebody definitely needs to transcribe a log of one of the explorations into SCP-015.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whee hee hee I'm replying to a necropost

In all seriousness and on-topic: this doesn't need an exploration log. It's perfect the way it is. Leaves just enough to the imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aaand I've just noticed this has reached +100! You've done it again, Gears. :)
